content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Wires the proposer into the dispatcher and collector orchestrators added in #3340 and #3439.

The pipeline now acts as the lifecycle coordinator: it starts dispatcher and collector tasks for each session, shares recovery behavior with the collector, and restarts both loops from chain-derived state when collector submission requires it.

## Behavior

- Runs one dispatcher task and one collector task per pipeline session.
- Dispatcher task recovers chain state, then delegates safe-head fan-out to `ProofDispatcher`.
- Collector task recovers chain state, then delegates polling, sequential submit, backlog draining, and retry-session handling to `ProofCollectorOrchestrator`.
- Submit failures, timeout, cancellation, task exits, task errors, and task panics restart the session instead of terminating `run()`.
- Adds the default inline submit timeout and wires it through service configuration.
- Keeps recovery in the pipeline so dispatcher and collector remain restart-safe from chain-derived state.

## Verification

- `cargo test -p base-proposer --lib`
- `cargo test -p base-proposer --lib --features metrics`
- `cargo clippy -p base-pr...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
### What changed? Why?

Extracts signer lifecycle orchestration out of the registrar driver into a dedicated `SignerManager`. The driver now focuses on fleet discovery, instance resolution, CRL gating, and loop control, while `SignerManager` owns signer proof task reconciliation, per-task cancellation, join outcome handling, and orphan signer cleanup.

This keeps long-running proof generation from blocking discovery and makes the registrar lifecycle boundaries clearer: discovery produces a per-cycle snapshot, the signer manager reconciles that snapshot against in-flight work, and orphan deregistration runs through the same signer lifecycle boundary.

### Notes to reviewers

The main review surface is the ownership split between `driver.rs` and `signer_manager.rs`. `RegistrationDriver` still owns resolution decisions, including health checks, attestation fetches, CRL checks, and the ok-to-deregister guard. `SignerManager` owns operations that submit signer registration or deregistration transactions.

The CRL path still uses `CertManager`, which is constructed with a cloned transaction manager from the signer manager so revocation transactions continue to use the same tx submissi...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
markdown
## Description

`RateTracker::rate_over` uses unchecked `u64` addition (`total + b`) for byte accumulation in its fold closure. Under sustained high throughput, the byte total within the 300-second sliding window can exceed `u64::MAX`, causing an arithmetic overflow a panic in debug builds and a silent wraparound (producing a wildly incorrect rate) in release builds.

## Change

`total + b` → `total.saturating_add(b)` so the accumulator saturates at `u64::MAX` instead of overflowing. The computed rate may be slightly inaccurate at saturation but will never crash or produce a nonsensical negative/zero-as-huge value.

## Impact

Low-probability but high severity in production under heavy DA load. The fix is trivial and localized to a single line.

Closes #3492

## Description

Prepares the consumer side for the fix described in #3274.

When flashblock execution re-produces different receipts than the builder
(23% divergence, ~8-14k gas gap on refund-heavy txs), consumers serve
incorrect pending logs and gas estimates.

This PR adds an optional `receipts` field to `Metadata` so that when the
builder includes receipts in the flashblock payload, consumers can use them
directly instead of re-executing eliminating the divergence entirely.

## Changes

- `crates/common/flashblocks/src/metadata.rs`: Added optional
  `receipts: Option<HashMap<B256, BaseTransactionReceipt>>` field
- `crates/execution/flashblocks/src/state_builder.rs`: Added
  `builder_receipts` field + `with_builder_receipts()` setter +
  `execute_with_builder_receipt()` method. Priority 1 in
  `execute_transaction()` is now builder-provided receipts.
- `crates/execution/flashblocks/src/processor.rs`: Passes metadata
  receipts to `PendingStateBuilder` in `build_pending_state()`.

## Compatibility

Backward compatible when `receipts` is `None` (old flashblocks), the
existing re-execution path is used unchanged.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
markdown
## Description

`OPSuccinctDataFetcher` panics when the L2 RPC returns `null` for a requested block or receipts. Three `unwrap()` calls on `Option` values crash the proposer/proof utility instead of returning a recoverable error.

## Changes

- `get_l2_block_data_range`: replaced `.unwrap()` on block and receipt fetches (lines 238, 240) with `.ok_or_else()` returning a contextual error with the block number
- `get_host_args`: replaced `.unwrap()` on the end-block fetch (line 765) with `.ok_or_else()`, matching the existing start-block error handling pattern on the same function

All three now return `Err(...)` with the missing block/receipt number instead of panicking. The caller can retry, mark the range failed, or surface the error without crashing the process.

Closes #3488

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Summary

Adds `RocksdbBatchSession` — a session type that accumulates proof storage writes for multiple blocks into a single `WriteBatch`, avoiding per-address fsync overhead during bulk sync.

Benchmarks show ~32% improvement on uncompacted RocksDB data and ~10% on compacted data for reverse history reads.

## Changes

- Export `RocksdbBatchSession` from `trie/src/db/mod.rs` and `trie/src/lib.rs`
- Add 42 new batch-write metrics to `metrics.rs`
- Update `initialize.rs` and `db/store.rs` to support batch session initialization
- Update `prune/pruner.rs` for minor compatibility
- Add `benches/deep_history_reads.rs` (+228 lines) — benchmarks uncompacted vs compacted RocksDB read patterns
- Update `benches/witness_reads.rs`

## Performance

Benchmark results (Base mainnet, ~20M blocks of proof history):
- Uncompacted reads: **~32% faster** vs reverse-seek baseline
- Compacted reads: **~10% faster** vs reverse-seek baseline

The speedup comes from complement-encoding block numbers in history table keys (`u64::MAX - block_number`), allowing forward `seek()+next()` instead of slow `seek_for_prev()+prev()`.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Bug

`createPolicyWithAccounts`, `updateAllowlist`, and `updateBlocklist` each carry an `address[]` parameter that is subject to a 64-account cap (`MAX_ACCOUNTS_PER_BATCH`), but the cap was only enforced after a full ABI decode that allocates the entire `Vec<Address>` on the heap. A caller could therefore trigger heap allocation of an arbitrarily large address array before receiving `BatchSizeTooLarge`.

## Fix

Added `check_batch_calldata_length` in `dispatch.rs` that reads the raw 32-byte array-length word at calldata offset `[100..132]` (4-byte selector + 3 x 32-byte head words) before any ABI decode occurs. If the encoded length exceeds `MAX_ACCOUNTS_PER_BATCH` (64), the function returns `BatchSizeTooLarge` immediately without allocating. The check is applied in `inner()` for all three affected selectors via a single selector match.

Also promotes `MAX_ACCOUNTS_PER_BATCH` to `pub` so it is accessible from dispatch and test modules without `pub(crate)` workarounds.

## References

- Cantina finding: https://cantina.xyz/code/73a6cf79-adb2-4d94-9e8b-3a5fdef7f64e/findings/29
- Linear ticket: https://linear.app/coinbase/issue/BOP-391

content_signals.body_excerpt:
[BOP-388](https://linear.app/coinbase/issue/BOP-388)

## Motivation

`parse_slot_value` called `int.to_string()` on a `LitInt`, which preserves Rust base prefixes (`0b`, `0o`) and type suffixes (`u64`, `usize`, etc.). The function then only checked for a `0x` prefix before passing the string to `U256::from_str_radix` with radix 10. This caused valid Rust integer literals like `0b101`, `0o17`, and `42u64` to be incorrectly rejected with a misleading "Invalid slot number" error.

Cantina finding: https://cantina.xyz/code/c75e122d-bfec-4090-8ae7-2f591169e2b1/findings/36

## What changed

- Strip any Rust integer type suffix (`u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`, `usize`, `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`, `isize`) from the raw `to_string()` output before inspecting the prefix.
- Dispatch to the correct `from_str_radix` radix based on prefix: `0x`/`0X` hex (16), `0b`/`0B` binary (2), `0o`/`0O` octal (8).
- Filter underscores before calling `from_str_radix` (ruint does not accept them in non-decimal inputs).
- Use `base10_digits()` for the decimal fallback, which already strips suffixes and underscore separators.
- Update the doc comment on `parse_slot_value` to list all four supported ...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## What

`TokenCreateParams::validate_stablecoin` was a no-op that delegated currency
validation to `B20StablecoinStorage::initialize`. Because the token existence
check (`already_deployed`) fires between `params.validate()` and `initialize()`,
a caller supplying an invalid currency (empty or non-uppercase) for an address
that is already occupied received `TokenAlreadyExists` instead of
`MissingRequiredField` or `InvalidCurrency`. This leaks whether a given
`(caller, salt)` address slot is already occupied.

## Fix

Implement the empty and non-A-Z currency checks directly in
`TokenCreateParams::validate_stablecoin` so they execute before the existence
check. The existing validation in `B20StablecoinStorage::initialize` is
unchanged -- currency is still validated there, so the behavior for new
deployments is unaffected. A regression test creates a stablecoin at a
`(caller, salt)` address, then retries with empty and non-uppercase currency
over the same address and asserts the currency errors are returned rather than
`TokenAlreadyExists`.

## References

- Cantina finding: https://cantina.xyz/code/73a6cf79-adb2-4d94-9e8b-3a5fdef7f64e/findings/31
- Linear ticket: https://linear.app...

## Summary

Adds `RocksdbBatchSession` — a session type that accumulates proof storage writes for multiple blocks into a single `WriteBatch`, avoiding per-address fsync overhead during bulk sync.

Benchmarks show ~32% improvement on uncompacted RocksDB data and ~10% on compacted data for reverse history reads.

## Performance

Benchmark results (Base mainnet, ~20M blocks of proof history):
- Uncompacted reads: **~32% faster** vs reverse-seek baseline
- Compacted reads: **~10% faster** vs reverse-seek baseline

The speedup comes from complement-encoding block numbers in history table keys (`u64::MAX - block_number`), allowing forward `seek()+next()` instead of slow `seek_for_prev()+prev()`.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
markdown
## Description

`RateTracker::rate_over` uses unchecked `u64` addition (`total + b`) for byte accumulation in its fold closure. Under sustained high throughput, the byte total within the 300-second sliding window can exceed `u64::MAX`, causing an arithmetic overflow a panic in debug builds and a silent wraparound (producing a wildly incorrect rate) in release builds.

## Change

`total + b` → `total.saturating_add(b)` so the accumulator saturates at `u64::MAX` instead of overflowing. The computed rate may be slightly inaccurate at saturation but will never crash or produce a nonsensical negative/zero-as-huge value.

## Impact

Low-probability but high severity in production under heavy DA load. The fix is trivial and localized to a single line.

Closes #3492

## Summary

Wires the proposer into the dispatcher and collector orchestrators added in #3340 and #3439.

The pipeline now acts as the lifecycle coordinator: it starts dispatcher and collector tasks for each session, shares recovery behavior with the collector, and restarts both loops from chain-derived state when collector submission requires it.

## Behavior

- Runs one dispatcher task and one collector task per pipeline session.
- Dispatcher task recovers chain state, then delegates safe-head fan-out to `ProofDispatcher`.
- Collector task recovers chain state, then delegates polling, sequential submit, backlog draining, and retry-session handling to `ProofCollectorOrchestrator`.
- Submit failures, timeout, cancellation, task exits, task errors, and task panics restart the session instead of terminating `run()`.
- Adds the default inline submit timeout and wires it through service configuration.
- Keeps recovery in the pipeline so dispatcher and collector remain restart-safe from chain-derived state.

### What changed? Why?

Extracts signer lifecycle orchestration out of the registrar driver into a dedicated `SignerManager`. The driver now focuses on fleet discovery, instance resolution, CRL gating, and loop control, while `SignerManager` owns signer proof task reconciliation, per-task cancellation, join outcome handling, and orphan signer cleanup.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## What

`TokenCreateParams::validate_stablecoin` was a no-op that delegated currency
validation to `B20StablecoinStorage::initialize`. Because the token existence
check (`already_deployed`) fires between `params.validate()` and `initialize()`,
a caller supplying an invalid currency (empty or non-uppercase) for an address
that is already occupied received `TokenAlreadyExists` instead of
`MissingRequiredField` or `InvalidCurrency`. This leaks whether a given
`(caller, salt)` address slot is already occupied.

## Fix

Implement the empty and non-A-Z currency checks directly in
`TokenCreateParams::validate_stablecoin` so they execute before the existence
check. The existing validation in `B20StablecoinStorage::initialize` is
unchanged -- currency is still validated there, so the behavior for new
deployments is unaffected. A regression test creates a stablecoin at a
`(caller, salt)` address, then retries with empty and non-uppercase currency
over the same address and asserts the currency errors are returned rather than
`TokenAlreadyExists`.

## References

- Cantina finding: https://cantina.xyz/code/73a6cf79-adb2-4d94-9e8b-3a5fdef7f64e/findings/31
- Linear ticket: https://linear.app...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
## Bug

`createPolicyWithAccounts`, `updateAllowlist`, and `updateBlocklist` each carry an `address[]` parameter that is subject to a 64-account cap (`MAX_ACCOUNTS_PER_BATCH`), but the cap was only enforced after a full ABI decode that allocates the entire `Vec<Address>` on the heap. A caller could therefore trigger heap allocation of an arbitrarily large address array before receiving `BatchSizeTooLarge`.

## Fix

Added `check_batch_calldata_length` in `dispatch.rs` that reads the raw 32-byte array-length word at calldata offset `[100..132]` (4-byte selector + 3 x 32-byte head words) before any ABI decode occurs. If the encoded length exceeds `MAX_ACCOUNTS_PER_BATCH` (64), the function returns `BatchSizeTooLarge` immediately without allocating. The check is applied in `inner()` for all three affected selectors via a single selector match.

Also promotes `MAX_ACCOUNTS_PER_BATCH` to `pub` so it is accessible from dispatch and test modules without `pub(crate)` workarounds.

## References

- Cantina finding: https://cantina.xyz/code/73a6cf79-adb2-4d94-9e8b-3a5fdef7f64e/findings/29
- Linear ticket: https://linear.app/coinbase/issue/BOP-391

## What

Adds a load-test workload that drives **standard ERC20 `transfer(...)` traffic** against a real, deployed Solidity contract.

The `erc20` workload type already existed (it produced `transfer(to, amount)` calldata), but there was **no token setup** — every sender held a zero balance, so transfers reverted and the run measured reverts rather than transfers. This adds the missing setup phase, mirroring the existing B-20 flow.

## How

- **Setup phase** (`runner/erc20.rs`): `needs_erc20_setup()` + `setup_erc20_tokens()` deploy a standard OpenZeppelin ERC20 (`LoadTestERC20`) via a `CREATE` tx (or reuse a configured address), mint `erc20_mint_amount` to every sender, resolve the address back into the workload, and rebuild the generator.
- **Contract**: `LoadTestERC20.sol` — OpenZeppelin `ERC20` with an open `mint` and a no-arg constructor. Bytecode is sourced via `base-test-utils` `sol!` bindings (`LoadTestERC20::BYTECODE`), matching the repo's existing convention (no committed build artifacts).
- **Config**: ERC20 `contract` is now optional (auto-deploy when omitted); new `erc20_mint_amount` knob; new `examples/erc20-devnet.yaml`.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
[BOP-388](https://linear.app/coinbase/issue/BOP-388)

## Motivation

`parse_slot_value` called `int.to_string()` on a `LitInt`, which preserves Rust base prefixes (`0b`, `0o`) and type suffixes (`u64`, `usize`, etc.). The function then only checked for a `0x` prefix before passing the string to `U256::from_str_radix` with radix 10. This caused valid Rust integer literals like `0b101`, `0o17`, and `42u64` to be incorrectly rejected with a misleading "Invalid slot number" error.

Cantina finding: https://cantina.xyz/code/c75e122d-bfec-4090-8ae7-2f591169e2b1/findings/36

## What changed

- Strip any Rust integer type suffix (`u8`, `u16`, `u32`, `u64`, `u128`, `usize`, `i8`, `i16`, `i32`, `i64`, `i128`, `isize`) from the raw `to_string()` output before inspecting the prefix.
- Dispatch to the correct `from_str_radix` radix based on prefix: `0x`/`0X` hex (16), `0b`/`0B` binary (2), `0o`/`0O` octal (8).
- Filter underscores before calling `from_str_radix` (ruint does not accept them in non-decimal inputs).
- Use `base10_digits()` for the decimal fallback, which already strips suffixes and underscore separators.
- Update the doc comment on `parse_slot_value` to list all four supported ...

## Description

`OPSuccinctDataFetcher` panics when the L2 RPC returns `null` for a requested block or receipts. Three `unwrap()` calls on `Option` values crash the proposer/proof utility instead of returning a recoverable error.

## Changes

- `get_l2_block_data_range`: replaced `.unwrap()` on block and receipt fetches (lines 238, 240) with `.ok_or_else()` returning a contextual error with the block number
- `get_host_args`: replaced `.unwrap()` on the end-block fetch (line 765) with `.ok_or_else()`, matching the existing start-block error handling pattern on the same function

All three now return `Err(...)` with the missing block/receipt number instead of panicking. The caller can retry, mark the range failed, or surface the error without crashing the process.

Closes #3488

content_signals.body_excerpt:
markdown
## Description

`RateTracker::rate_over` uses unchecked `u64` addition (`total + b`) for byte accumulation in its fold closure. Under sustained high throughput, the byte total within the 300-second sliding window can exceed `u64::MAX`, causing an arithmetic overflow a panic in debug builds and a silent wraparound (producing a wildly incorrect rate) in release builds.

## Change

`total + b` → `total.saturating_add(b)` so the accumulator saturates at `u64::MAX` instead of overflowing. The computed rate may be slightly inaccurate at saturation but will never crash or produce a nonsensical negative/zero-as-huge value.

## Impact

Low-probability but high severity in production under heavy DA load. The fix is trivial and localized to a single line.

Closes #3492

Adds `RocksdbBatchSession` — a session type that accumulates proof storage writes for multiple blocks into a single `WriteBatch`, avoiding per-address fsync overhead during bulk sync.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
Fixes https://github.com/ethereum-optimism/optimism-private/issues/553

## Summary

A **fork-activation block** — the first L2 block at/after a fork's activation timestamp — must contain only deposit transactions (the L1-attributes deposit + the network-upgrade automatic deposits), no user transactions. op-geth enforces this at the executor / consensus-validity layer (`CalcDAFootprint`), but the shared Rust executor (`alloy-op-evm`, used by **both op-reth and kona-client**) was missing it — a latent consensus-validity divergence where op-reth / kona-client would *accept* an activation block containing user txs that op-geth *rejects*.

No live exploit: Jovian's canonical activation block on OP Mainnet contains no user txs. Also, the batch validity rule is already there, so it's enforced for the safe chain already.

## What this does

- Adds a fork-activation-block guard to the shared `OpBlockExecutor`: on the first block of a fork at or after Jovian, any non-deposit (user) tx is rejected with `OpBlockExecutionError::UnexpectedNonDepositTxInForkActivationBlock`.
- Adds `OpHardforks::is_no_user_tx_activation_block(parent_ts, block_ts)` to `alloy-op-hardforks`: a block is the first ...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
Fix manual CircleCI UI/API dispatches for the dynamic continuation pipeline.

CircleCI validates explicitly supplied setup parameters against the continuation config too. The continuation config consumes transformed `c-*` parameters, but it still needs unused passthrough declarations for original UI/API parameter names like `fault_proofs_dispatch`; otherwise manual runs fail with `Unexpected argument(s)` before workflows start.

This adds those passthrough declarations and a setup-time check that compares setup parameters against the merged continuation config so future refactors fail before calling the continuation API.

Validated with:
- `mise exec -- bash .circleci/scripts/test-decision-tree.sh`
- `mise exec -- bash -lc '.circleci/scripts/merge-configs.sh && .circleci/scripts/test-continuation-params.sh'`\n- `mise exec -- shellcheck .circleci/scripts/test-continuation-params.sh`\n- `git diff --check`

## Description

Adds `.claude/skills/update-reth/` — a thin agent workflow for bumping the pinned upstream reth dependency. The skill deliberately repeats none of the procedure: `rust/UPDATING-RETH.md` stays the single source of truth, and the skill just orchestrates (orient → isolate in a worktree → execute the guide → verify → reviewed PR → fold learnings back).

The durable learnings from the v2.3.0 bump (#21348) move into the guide itself instead of living in the skill:

- a new **current-pin audit** step: when the existing pin isn't an ancestor of the target, verify what it carried is contained in or superseded by the target before moving
- the **consensus-adjacent adaptation rule**: derive gas-accounting changes as (old upstream default → new upstream default) applied onto our override, with a red/green-verified test for any new semantic branch — even fork-gated ones
- the **stable-toolchain `cargo fmt` trap** (only the `just` recipes use the pinned nightly)

Cross-referenced from `docs/ai/rust-dev.md`.

## Tests

Docs-only change; reviewed locally via tuicr.

## Root cause

The SDM warming inspector (`alloy-op-evm/src/post_exec/inspector.rs`) maintains **block-scoped** `warmed_accounts`/`warmed_slots` maps. These are mutated during EVM execution inside `execute_transaction_without_commit` — **before** the commit decision — and they aren't part of the journaled state, so discarding a transaction's changes does **not** roll them back.

op-rbuilder's payload builder selects transactions with `execute_transaction_with_commit_condition` and declines candidates (`CommitChanges::No`) that exceed a builder gas/DA/address limit or are reverted-and-excluded. Those declined candidates still warmed the maps, so a later *committed* tx could claim a block-warming refund attributed to a transaction that never entered the block. The canonical single-pass paths — block import and `debug_replaySDMBlock` derivation — only ever `execute_transaction` (commit-only), so they never see that phantom warmth and derive a strictly smaller refund set. Hence the producer's payload had more `SDMGasEntry`s than derivation reproduced.

content_signals.body_excerpt:
Fixes https://github.com/ethereum-optimism/optimism-private/issues/553

## Summary

A **fork-activation block** — the first L2 block at/after a fork's activation timestamp — must contain only deposit transactions (the L1-attributes deposit + the network-upgrade automatic deposits), no user transactions. op-geth enforces this at the executor / consensus-validity layer (`CalcDAFootprint`), but the shared Rust executor (`alloy-op-evm`, used by **both op-reth and kona-client**) was missing it — a latent consensus-validity divergence where op-reth / kona-client would *accept* an activation block containing user txs that op-geth *rejects*.

No live exploit: Jovian's canonical activation block on OP Mainnet contains no user txs. Also, the batch validity rule is already there, so it's enforced for the safe chain already.

## What this does

- Adds a fork-activation-block guard to the shared `OpBlockExecutor`: on the first block of a fork at or after Jovian, any non-deposit (user) tx is rejected with `OpBlockExecutionError::UnexpectedNonDepositTxInForkActivationBlock`.
- Adds `OpHardforks::is_no_user_tx_activation_block(parent_ts, block_ts)` to `alloy-op-hardforks`: a block is the first ...

content_signals.body_excerpt:
Fix manual CircleCI UI/API dispatches for the dynamic continuation pipeline.

CircleCI validates explicitly supplied setup parameters against the continuation config too. The continuation config consumes transformed `c-*` parameters, but it still needs unused passthrough declarations for original UI/API parameter names like `fault_proofs_dispatch`; otherwise manual runs fail with `Unexpected argument(s)` before workflows start.

This adds those passthrough declarations and a setup-time check that compares setup parameters against the merged continuation config so future refactors fail before calling the continuation API.

Validated with:
- `mise exec -- bash .circleci/scripts/test-decision-tree.sh`
- `mise exec -- bash -lc '.circleci/scripts/merge-configs.sh && .circleci/scripts/test-continuation-params.sh'`\n- `mise exec -- shellcheck .circleci/scripts/test-continuation-params.sh`\n- `git diff --check`

## Description

Adds `.claude/skills/update-reth/` — a thin agent workflow for bumping the pinned upstream reth dependency. The skill deliberately repeats none of the procedure: `rust/UPDATING-RETH.md` stays the single source of truth, and the skill just orchestrates (orient → isolate in a worktree → execute the guide → verify → reviewed PR → fold learnings back).

The durable learnings from the v2.3.0 bump (#21348) move into the guide itself instead of living in the skill:

- a new **current-pin audit** step: when the existing pin isn't an ancestor of the target, verify what it carried is contained in or superseded by the target before moving
- the **consensus-adjacent adaptation rule**: derive gas-accounting changes as (old upstream default → new upstream default) applied onto our override, with a red/green-verified test for any new semantic branch — even fork-gated ones
- the **stable-toolchain `cargo fmt` trap** (only the `just` recipes use the pinned nightly)

Cross-referenced from `docs/ai/rust-dev.md`.

## Tests

Docs-only change; reviewed locally via tuicr.
